title: Geometric and Topological Obstructions to Hermitianization in Quasi-Hermitian Quantum Systems
authors: Ming-Zhang Wang, Xu-Yang Hou, Hao Guo
abstract: Quasi-Hermitian quantum systems, including $\mathcal{PT}$-symmetric ones, can be mapped to equivalent Hermitian systems via a similarity transformation that redefines the inner product with a positive-definite metric operator. Although an instantaneous algebraic Hermitianization can be obtained locally from a positive metric operator, a stronger requirement is needed for dynamical equivalence: the similarity transformation must be proper, globally single-valued, and compatible with the modified quasi-Hermitian Schrodinger equation. We identify two distinct obstructions: geometric obstructions arising from the curvature of a metric-induced connection, and topological obstructions originating from non-trivial holonomies around non-contractible loops in parameter space. We derive explicit criteria for these obstructions and illustrate them with concrete examples. Our results establish a geometric and topological foundation for the Hermitianization of quasi-Hermitian systems, clarifying when they can be globally reduced to Hermitian ones and when intrinsic non-Hermitian features persist.
summary: This paper identifies the geometric and topological reasons why a non-Hermitian system with a real spectrum cannot always be globally mapped to a Hermitian one. It demonstrates that even if a local mapping exists, curvature and non-trivial loops in parameter space can prevent a consistent global description. This establishes a foundation for understanding the persistence of intrinsic non-Hermitian features.
main_problem: The paper investigates the conditions under which a quasi-Hermitian quantum system can be globally and dynamically mapped to an equivalent Hermitian system via a similarity transformation.
main_result: The authors identify two distinct obstructions to global Hermitianization: geometric obstructions arising from non-zero gauge curvature and topological obstructions arising from non-trivial holonomies (Wilson loops) in the parameter space.
method: The study employs a gauge theory approach, using differential geometry to analyze the metric-induced gauge connection, curvature, and Wilson loops to determine the integrability of the similarity transformation.
model_or_system: The study focuses on finite-dimensional quasi-Hermitian quantum systems (including PT-symmetric ones) with a real energy spectrum, characterized by a parameter-dependent Hamiltonian H(R) and a positive-definite metric operator eta(R).
key_observables: The Berry phase (and its mismatch between quasi-Hermitian and Hermitian descriptions), gauge curvature (F_G), and the Wilson loop (W(C)).
important_parameters: External parameters R defining the system; the regime of positive-definite metric operators and entirely real energy spectra.
main_assumptions: The energy spectrum of the Hamiltonian is assumed to be entirely real, and the metric operator is assumed to be positive-definite.
figures_summary: Figure 2 illustrates a topological obstruction, showing how loops enclosing a hole in parameter space result in different Berry phases compared to loops that avoid the hole.
paper_structure: The paper defines the framework of quasi-Hermitian mechanics, establishes the criteria for dynamical compatibility, classifies geometric and topological obstructions using gauge theory, and provides three illustrative examples (integrable, geometric obstruction, and topological obstruction).
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and non-Hermitian physics, as it provides a fundamental mathematical framework for understanding when non-Hermitian dynamics can be truly treated as equivalent to standard Hermitian dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Geometric obstruction: Left, quasi-Hermitian parameter space with loops at r0 = 2 √

Figure 2. Topological obstruction: Left, quasi-Hermitian parameter space with a hole; right, Hermitian parameter space also with a hole. The red loops enclose the holes, leading to non-trivial Wilson loops and the absence of a global proper S. The green loops avoid the holes, giving trivial Wilson loops and preserving the local equivalence.
